quote: This is Bucky with ION reaching out about the solar form you guys filled out online. How you doing?
stewart_read: The rep uses the standard form-fill intro on what is almost certainly a recycled lead — Ken immediately discloses an existing system and a prior Ion quote, suggesting this is not a first-touch organic lead. The intro is technically compliant but contextually mismatched. No harm done at this moment, but it signals the rep did not review lead history before dialing.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may not have had lead-type metadata available before the call — a system gap rather than a rep gap.
coaching_implication: Use this as a lead-type awareness moment. If the CRM shows prior Ion contact, the rep should open with a recycled-lead acknowledgment rather than a fresh form-fill intro. Confirm with manager whether lead-type metadata is visible to setters before dialing.

quote: I think I got a quote kinda over the phone. But Okay.
stewart_read: Ken's first disclosure of a prior Ion quote is the moment the rep needed to investigate: who quoted it, what was the number, what did Ken think of it. Instead the rep responds with 'Okay' and pivots immediately to contract-status. This is the root cause of the 'duplicative' objection at 03:10 — the rep had the information and didn't use it. Strategic read rejected: there is no later move the rep sets up here; the pivot to contract-status is a checklist move, not a setup.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been prioritizing DQ-check (contract status) before investing in the prior-quote conversation — a defensible triage if done quickly. But the rep never returns to investigate the quote, so the strategic read does not hold.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, replay 02:03–02:17. Ask the rep: 'What did you learn about the prior quote here?' The answer is nothing. The coaching move is: when a customer discloses a prior Ion contact, pause the checklist and ask one question — 'What did you think of it?' That answer shapes everything that follows.

quote: I already talked to somebody about and they gave me a quote. So I I don't know. This is kinda duplicative.
stewart_read: Ken's 'duplicative' objection is the second surface of the prior-quote concern — and the rep's second miss. The codex rebuttal for 'already saw a design' calls for comparison framing: 'it's always best to get a couple looks so you have something to compare against.' The rep instead offers a hold-and-transfer with no reframe. The objection was predictable from 02:03 and the rep had the tool to handle it.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep's hold-and-transfer could be read as routing to a specialist who can handle the comparison conversation more credibly. But the rep offers no reframe before the hold, so Ken goes on hold still feeling the call is duplicative — the specialist inherits an unresolved objection.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the codex rebuttal for 'already_saw_design.' The move before any hold is a one-sentence reframe: 'It's always worth a second look — we may quote it differently, and worst case you confirm you got a good deal the first time.' That reframe changes what the specialist inherits.

quote: It was on the high side, I thought.
stewart_read: Ken's price objection on the prior quote is a flip opportunity the rep walks past entirely. The rep responds with a hold offer rather than inverting the objection. The inversion available here: Ken's existing system is 15+ years old, his power goes out frequently, and he's already paying for a system that doesn't give him backup — the prior quote being 'high' is the cost of not solving the problem. The rep had the emotional material to make this move and didn't.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have assessed that Ken's price sensitivity made a second quote unlikely to land and routed to a specialist as the higher-percentage play. But the rep made no attempt at the flip before routing, so the strategic read is unsupported.
coaching_implication: This is the objection_inversion coaching moment for this call. In the 1-on-1: 'Ken told you the prior quote was high. What did you do with that?' Walk the rep through the flip structure — the pain of frequent outages + an aging system is the cost frame, not the Powerwall price. The rep doesn't need a number to make this move; they need to redirect to the problem.

quote: our power goes off quite often. We're, in a rural residential area, so, we're susceptible to having the lines go down.
stewart_read: Ken volunteers a specific, emotionally loaded use case for battery backup — frequent rural outages. This is the anchor for the entire pitch and the rep never returns to it. The outage pain is the reason Ken filled out the form, the reason he took a prior quote, and the reason he's still on the phone. It should have been the emotional center of every reframe that followed.
coaching_implication: The rep captured this signal and then set it down. In the 1-on-1: 'Ken told you his power goes out often and he lives in a rural area. Where did that show up in your pitch?' It didn't. The coaching move: enthusiasm signals are anchors, not asides. Every reframe after 01:41 should have tied back to 'you said your power goes out a lot.'

stewart_read: Ken's disclosure that his existing system is SolarCity — now Tesla Solar — is an orphaned_flow data point the rep does not capture systematically. The codex orphaned flow asks: own or lease, original company, panel size, system working. The rep gets panel size (10kW) and original company (SolarCity) organically but never asks own-vs-lease or system-working status. System age (15+ years, installed ~18 years after move-in per Ken's math) is also a relevant data point for the specialist. The rep routes to a specialist without completing the orphaned flow.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that the specialist would gather orphaned-flow details directly. But the specialist then DQs on kilowatt size — a ruling that might have been challenged or contextualized if the rep had completed the flow and surfaced own-vs-lease status.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through the orphaned_flow checklist. Four questions, 60 seconds. The rep got two of four organically and stopped. The missing data (own vs lease, system working) is exactly what the specialist needed to make an accurate ruling — and potentially to avoid a wrongful DQ.

quote: I was trying to get a specialist to come talk to you, but I was really fighting for you. But they they said your your kilowatt size is too big, and it's too big for a battery or a backup.
stewart_read: The DQ delivery is the call's lowest moment. Ken arrived warm, disclosed a genuine pain (frequent outages), and stayed patient through a hold. The rep delivers the DQ as a verbatim relay of a specialist ruling with no empathy bridge, no acknowledgment of Ken's outage problem, and no alternative path. 'I'm sorry, man. There's nothing I could have done' is self-focused — it centers the rep's effort, not Ken's unresolved need. The codex calls for acknowledge + bridge to value; the rep delivers neither.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have had no alternative to offer if the DQ was legitimate. But even a legitimate DQ warrants an empathy bridge and a 'here's what you could explore' close — the rep offered nothing.
coaching_implication: This is the empathy delivery coaching moment. In the 1-on-1: 'When you told Ken the specialist said no, what did you give him to do next?' Nothing. A suggested rephrase direction (not verbatim): acknowledge the outage problem specifically, then offer a concrete next step — whether that's a referral, a future-touch when product availability changes, or a roofing conversation if the system age creates a replacement path. The close should serve Ken, not apologize for the rep.

quote: I tried, though. I tried it. But, man, let's just do it. Here we go.
stewart_read: The rep's closing line is directed at themselves, not at Ken — 'let's just do it. Here we go' reads as self-talk about ending the call. Ken has already said 'Alright. Okay. Thanks. Bye.' The rep's final words add nothing for Ken and underscore the absence of any forward path, referral, or warmth recovery. The call ends on the rep's frustration, not on Ken's experience.
coaching_implication: Minor but worth a brief mention in the 1-on-1: the close of a DQ call is still a brand moment. Ken may call back, refer a neighbor, or be re-contacted on a future product. 'I tried, though' is not the last impression Ion wants to leave. A clean, warm exit — 'I'm sorry we couldn't make it work today, Ken. If anything changes on our end I'll make sure you hear about it' — costs nothing and protects the relationship.
